petitioner_background: The petitioner is a painter from Nepal named Sunil Sharma who sought classification as an employment-based immigrant under section 203(b)(1)(A) of the INA as an alien of extraordinary ability in the arts. He submitted evidence of exhibitions in Nepal, Japan, and the United States, purported prizes from various exhibitions in Nepal, newspaper articles, seven letters of recommendation, and an unpublished portfolio of his paintings. He is described in recommendation letters as a young artist with future potential, and his work focuses on Nepalese art and ethnicity.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Petitioner submitted certificates and other evidence of 12 purported prizes or awards for his painting in Nepal, nine of which were documented by materials in a foreign language with uncertified English translations. One certificate entitled 'Bicycle Rally '89' was in English. Three exhibition brochures in English documented three prizes: (1) the '27th National Art and Craft Exhibition (2053)' with a 'Contemporary Art Consolation Prize' including a statement from Uttam Nepali of the Royal Nepal Academy Faculty of Fine Arts and Crafts; (2) the '30th National Art and Craft Exhibition 2056' organized by the Nepal Association of Fine Art (NAFA) at the National Birendra Art Gallery, where he won first prize in the Traditional Arts category, with an introductory essay by Chairman Vijay Thapa; and (3) a 'Paintings Exhibition' organized by the Commercial Artists' Association of Nepal (CAAN) held at Art Council Babarmahal in Kathmandu on August 17, 2001, where he received first prize, with an introduction by CAAN Central President I.B. Malla stating CAAN has approximately 15,000 members.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found that without certified translations of the foreign language documents, it could not determine whether the evidence supported the petitioner's claim per 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(3). The Bicycle Rally '89 certificate had no documentation connecting a bicycle rally over 14 years before filing to his work as an artist. For the three English-documented prizes, the statement from Uttam Nepali did not explain the significance of the consolation prize. The dates of the 27th and 30th National Art and Craft Exhibitions were stated in a non-Gregorian calendar without translation, preventing determination of whether the prizes demonstrated sustained acclaim. The CAAN brochure introduction was partially cut off. The record contained no critical reviews or national media coverage of the exhibitions or prize-winning works, and none of the recommendation letters discussed the petitioner's receipt of these awards. The AAO concluded the record did not demonstrate the NAFA and CAAN prizes were nationally recognized prizes reflecting sustained national or international acclaim.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Petitioner submitted a copy of his 'Artist Identity Card' issued by the Artists' Society of Nepal, identifying him as a 'Gen. Member' in the category of 'Con. Art' and valid to 'Sawan 2060.' Petitioner did not claim eligibility under this criterion on appeal.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The record contained no evidence of the Society's membership criteria or any other evidence that outstanding achievements are prerequisite to Society membership. Additionally, without translation of the date 'Sawan 2060' into the Gregorian calendar, the AAO could not determine if the membership was consistent with sustained acclaim.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Petitioner initially submitted six newspaper articles purportedly about him and his work, all printed in foreign language with uncertified English translations. On appeal, counsel contended three were published in leading national Nepalese periodicals and submitted additional evidence about the periodicals. Also on appeal, petitioner submitted three additional newspaper articles reviewing a restaurant, where the petitioner's painting of the goddess Kumari was seen in the background of photographs accompanying the reviews.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: Without certified translations per 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(3), the AAO could not determine whether the articles supported the petitioner's claim. The three restaurant review articles did not meet the criterion because they were not about the petitioner or his work, and because they were published after the petition was filed. Per 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(12) and Matter of Katigbak, the petitioner must establish eligibility at the time of filing.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Petitioner submitted seven letters of recommendation, two of which had uncertified English translations. Five letters from individuals involved with fine arts in Nepal were considered: (1) Mr. Mishra, another Nepalese artist, describing the petitioner as 'highly energetic, serious to discipline' and wishing him a successful exhibition; (2) the Artistic Director of the Aarohan Theatre Group in Kathmandu, stating the petitioner taught street children at the Aarohan hostel through a partnership with UNICEF from 1994 to 1995 and designed posters for five Aarohan productions; (3) a Nepalese artist discussing difficulties faced by modern artists in Nepal and praising the petitioner's efforts to conserve and promote Nepalese arts through self-study; (4) a letter from the Nepal Artists' Society describing 'marvelous compositions' in the petitioner's work; and (5) a letter from NAFA stating the petitioner has woven 'himself to be a milestone in the world of young artists.' Also a letter from the Durga Gallery affirming his efforts to promote Nepalese art internationally. Counsel asserted petitioner had been recognized outside Nepal through invitations to display work in India, Japan, and the United States.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The two letters with uncertified translations could not be considered per 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(3). While the statute and regulation do not require international recognition, the record did not demonstrate the petitioner was recognized for making original artistic contributions of major significance in Nepal in a manner consistent with sustained national acclaim. The AAO noted solicited letters cannot by themselves establish eligibility because they do not demonstrate work is of major significance beyond those with whom the alien worked directly, and solicited letters carry less weight than preexisting independent evidence. The letters did not describe specific major contributions; the Aarohan teaching attested to commendable teaching but did not establish original major contribution; the poster designs had no evidence of being recognized for original major contribution. Most letters described the petitioner as a young artist and discussed future potential rather than past accomplishments. The record contained no evidence the petitioner significantly influenced other visual artists in Nepal or abroad or otherwise had major impact on his field.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Record documented eight exhibitions of petitioner's work in Nepal between 1995 and 2001, one solo exhibition at the Japanese Tea House in Tokyo in 2000, and one solo show at the Artists' Museum in Washington, DC (now known as Apex Gallery) on November 2-29, 2002. Petitioner submitted programs and postcards from Nepal and Japan exhibitions, an exhibition brochure from Japan with an introduction praising his work, a 'Rental Exhibition Contract' between petitioner and the Artists' Museum showing total cost of $2,100, a printout from the museum's website, an undated letter from Rajendra Oli (President of the America Nepal Society) about his plan to tour Nepali communities, an October 31, 2002 letter from the Royal Nepalese Embassy requesting U.S. Embassy assistance, and an article from Spacetime with uncertified translation. On appeal, counsel cited exhibitions at the Artists' Museum, the Nepalese Embassy, and the Lalit Festival, with a foreword by the Co-chairman stating 47 leading artists from Nepal joined the exhibition.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found that frequent display of artwork is inherent to the petitioner's profession, and activities nominally falling under a criterion do not demonstrate acclaim if they are inherent or routine in the occupation. The record contained no evidence of the significance of the Nepal and Japan exhibitions beyond programs and postcards, no evidence the Japanese Tea House is a prestigious venue, and no critical acclaim or media coverage. The Artists' Museum exhibition was procured through payment by the artist ($2,100 rental contract), and an exhibition procured through payment does not demonstrate sustained acclaim; the only selection criteria was 'prior review and acceptance' and there was no evidence it was an exclusive or prestigious venue. The Royal Nepalese Embassy letter did not confirm the Ambassador would inaugurate the exhibition. The Spacetime article lacked a certified translation. There was no evidence petitioner exhibited at the Nepalese Embassy. The Lalit Festival foreword did not state artists were invited or that exhibition was by invitation only. Additional evidence about a cultural festival submitted on appeal could not be considered because it arose after the petition was filed per Katigbak.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Petitioner initially submitted no evidence relevant to this criterion. On appeal, counsel claimed petitioner satisfies this category and submitted evidence of sales of the petitioner's artwork.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO could not consider this evidence because it arose after the petition was filed; a petition cannot be approved at a future date after the petitioner becomes eligible under a new set of facts, per 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(12) and Matter of Katigbak.

outcome_reasoning: The AAO dismissed the appeal because the evidence did not establish that the petitioner had achieved sustained national or international acclaim as an artist placing him at the very top of his field at the time of filing. The majority of documents were submitted with uncertified translations and therefore could not be accorded weight under 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(3). Much of the evidence submitted on appeal arose after the petition was filed and could not be considered under Matter of Katigbak. The petitioner failed to meet any of the regulatory criteria discussed, and the burden of proof in visa petition proceedings remained entirely with the petitioner per Section 291 of the Act.
legal_citations: Section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Immigration and Nationality Act, 8 U.S.C. § 1153(b)(1)(A)
fraud_or_procedural_issues: Significant procedural issues with evidence: the majority of documents submitted with the petition were in a foreign language and were accompanied by uncertified English translations, violating 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(3), which requires a full English language translation certified as complete and accurate by a translator competent to translate from the foreign language into English. As a result, this evidence was not probative and was not accorded any weight. Additionally, much of the evidence submitted on appeal arose after the petition was filed and could not be considered under 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(12) and Matter of Katigbak. An uncertified translation of a Spacetime article misstated the date of an exhibition as November 17, 2003.
